behaviors.description: The conversation history passed to the LLM is trimmed to the most recent 10 messages when the full history exceeds the context window.
behaviors.justification: This is a cross-module handoff check (history manager/prompt builder -> LLM client). Unit tests for trimming logic alone can miss wiring bugs where untrimmed history is still sent. No explicit real external contract or round-trip external side effect is required, so System is not required. No dependence on model output quality, so Agentic is not required.
behaviors.test_description: Run the request pipeline with a 15+ message history, stub the LLM endpoint/client, and assert the outbound LLM payload contains only the last 10 messages in correct order.

behaviors.description: The generated response addresses the customer's question rather than producing a generic or off-topic reply.
behaviors.justification: Correctness depends on real LLM judgment/output quality (relevance/off-topic detection). A deterministic stub would hide this failure mode. Workflow was considered, but the behavior text only requires quality of a generated response, not validation of multiple agentic decisions in sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Use a representative prompt set against the real model and score whether responses directly answer the asked question (human rubric or calibrated evaluator), failing on generic/off-topic outputs above threshold.

behaviors.description: The tone re-check correctly identifies when a response is too formal for a casual-tier customer and triggers regeneration.
behaviors.justification: The key assertion is semantic tone identification ('too formal for casual-tier'), which depends on model judgment. Mocks can verify trigger plumbing but not classification correctness. Workflow was considered, but behavior text does not explicitly require validating end-to-end quality across multiple model decisions; it requires correct identification plus regeneration trigger.
behaviors.test_description: Feed casual-tier cases with intentionally formal candidate responses through the real tone-check step and assert it flags them and initiates regeneration; include controls that should not trigger.

behaviors.description: The full exchange (user message, generated response, any regenerations) is persisted to the database with correct timestamps.
behaviors.justification: This is multi-module state propagation into persistence (chat flow -> logging/persistence layer -> DB) and is best checked with a real local DB integration. System was considered, but behavior text targets internal DB persistence and timestamp correctness, not an explicit external API contract or external readback boundary.
behaviors.test_description: Execute a flow with at least one regeneration using the real persistence stack against a test DB, then query records and assert all entries exist (user, initial draft, regenerated response) with correct ordering and timestamps.

behaviors.description: When the tone check triggers regeneration, the second response replaces the first — the customer never sees the rejected draft.
behaviors.justification: This is an externally visible side-effect requirement ('customer never sees'). It requires round-trip verification at the user-visible boundary (e.g., real HTTP/UI response), not just internal function state. Agentic was considered, but behavior text is about replacement/visibility semantics, not model-output quality.
behaviors.test_description: Run an end-to-end request through the real serving boundary (HTTP/API), force tone-triggered regeneration, and assert the client-visible response body contains only the regenerated reply while the rejected draft is absent.
